description.eng:
if you lose face, you do something which makes you seem weak, stupid etc, and which makes people respect you less:
 He doesn't want to back down (=accept defeat in an argument) and risk losing face.

to deliberately ignore something that you know should not be happening:
 Teachers were turning a blind eye to smoking in school.
if something happens out of the blue, it is very unexpected

used to talk about a situation when something is far away from you in space or time:
in the distance
 Church bells rang in the distance (=they were far away).

if you do something without fail, you always do it:
 Tim visits his mother every day without fail.

used to say what sb can do if nothing else they have done is successful.
If all else fails, I'll have to sell the car.

used to say that you are not certain about sth because other things have to be considered.
Do you wanna go out? ~That depends.

words.eng: keep/ stay/ steer clear (of somebody/ something)

description.eng:
to avoid someone or something because of possible danger or trouble:
 If you're a beginner, steer clear of resorts with reputations for difficult skiing.

words.eng: what/ why/ how etc on earth...? spoken

description.eng:
used to ask a question when you are very surprised or angry:
 What on earth did you do that for?

words.eng: be in a state/ get into a stateBrE spoken

description.eng:
to be or become very nervous, anxious, or excited:
 Mum and Dad were in a right state when I got in.

words.eng:
make a lot of sense
a lot of here adds emphasis

description.eng:
to be a sensible thing to do:
it makes sense (for somebody) to do something
 It makes sense to save money while you can.
 Would it make sense for the city authorities to further restrict parking?

used to ask someone why or how something happened:
 How come you've ended up here?
 'Last I heard, she was teaching in Mexico.' 'How come?'

very much, very quickly, or with a lot of energy:
 I caught my thumb in the door and it hurt like mad.
 She ran like mad to catch the bus.

words.eng: take advantage of something (to do something)

description.eng:
to use a particular situation to do or get what you want:
 I took advantage of the good weather to paint the shed.
 You'll want to take full advantage of the beach-front clubs.

to change your decision, plan, or opinion about something:
 Her father tried to get her to change her mind.

to have no success or make no progress:
 The proposal went nowhere in the Senate.
get nowhere with somebody/ something
 He was getting nowhere with the Bentley case.
get somebody nowhere
 A negative attitude will get you nowhere.
 I soon realized that being tough was getting me nowhere fast (=was not helping me achieve anything).

used to say what someone should or should not do, especially because of rules or what someone in authority has said:
 We're supposed to check out of the hotel by 11 o'clock.
 I'm not supposed to tell anyone.
 What time are you supposed to be there?

used to show that you are very glad about something:
 Thank God that's over! I've never been so nervous in my life!
thank for
 'Only ten miles to go.' 'Thank heavens for that!'

used to say you do not know the answer to something:
 'Where's she gone then?' 'Don't ask me!'

to pretend that something is less serious than it really is by laughing or joking about it:
 Knox laughed off rumors that he would be running for mayor.

to succeed in speaking to someone on the telephone:
 I tried phoning her office, but I couldn't get through.
get through to
 At last I managed to get through to one of the managers.

description.eng:
very early in the morning:

almost:
 a place where the ground was more or less flat

used to say that even if something happens late or someone arrives late, this is better than it not happening or their not arriving at all
used when someone has said too confidently that they can do something or that something will happen

words.eng: a stone's throw from something/ away (from something)

description.eng:
very close to something:

words.eng: be a piece of cake spoken

description.eng:
to be very easy:

the position of having control or responsibility for a group of people or an activity:
in charge (of something)

 the officer in charge of the investigation

if that is the situation:

used to say you do not know the answer to something:
 'Where's she gone then?' 'Don't ask me!'

used to show that you are very glad about something:
 Thank God that's over! I've never been so nervous in my life!

to pretend that something is less serious than it really is by laughing or joking about it:
 Knox laughed off rumors that he would be running for mayor.

to reduce the amount, size, cost etc of something:
cut back on

cut something ↔ back

 Richer countries must do more to cut back carbon emissions.

continue driving
we drove on for several kilometers

a)FAIL
without success in spite of your efforts:

a)PUBLICIZE/MAKE KNOWN
to tell everyone about something that was secret:
public on/ with

used to emphasize your own opinion:
 He's just plain crazy, if you ask me.

a)
to attract someone's attention and make them look at something:
 Out on the freeway, a billboard caught his eye.
b)
to look at someone at the same moment that they are looking at you:
 Every time she caught his eye, she would glance away embarrassed.

(=have difficulty breathing after running, hurrying etc)
Andrew hurried in, slightly out of breath.

beginning used to emphasize that something has just begun, especially when you do not know how it will develop It's too early to say what will happen.
 It's early days yet. I don't want to make any predictions

not now, but probably soon:
 I can't leave just yet. I've still got a couple of letters to write.

used to say that it is lucky or good that something has happened:
 It's a good thing we brought some food with us.

if a situation is looking up, it is improving
SYN improve,
get better
 Now the summer's here things are looking up!

especially BrE to continue doing something:
 Sorry, I interrupted you. Please carry on.
carry on doing something
 You'll have an accident if you carry on driving like that.
carry on with
 I want to carry on with my course.
carry on as usual/ as you are/ regardless etc

lessons.title:
Oxford Word Skills, Idioms & Phrasal verb, Intermediate
by Ruth Gairns & StuartRedman (complete set) Flashcards - Lesson 3

description.eng:
to continue doing something, or to do something many times:
keep (on) doing something
 You just have to keep on trying.

continue doing something to continue doing something:
get on with
 Be quiet and get on with your work!

to continue to do a job or to study after the usual or expected time for leaving:
 He resigned as chairman, but stayed on as an instructor.

to go to an airport, train station etc to say goodbye to someone:
 They've gone to the airport to see their son off.

a)
if a place is cut off, people cannot leave it or reach it:
 In winter, the town is often cut off by snow.
b)
to be a long way from other places and be difficult to get to:
 Accessible only by air, the town is cut off from the rest of the country.
c)
if someone is cut off, they are lonely and not able to meet many other people:
 Many older people feel cut off and isolated.

leave to leave a place, especially in order to do something:
 John decided to go off on his own.
go off to
 He went off to work as usual.
go off to do something
 Geoff went off to play golf.

words.eng: mess around with somebody/ something (alsomess about with somebody/ something BrE) phrasal verb informal

description.eng:
1to have a sexual relationship with someone that you should not have a sexual relationship with:
 She'd been messing around with another man.
2to spend time playing with something, repairing it etc:
 Dave likes messing around with old cars.
3to use something and make annoying changes to it:
 Who's been messing around with my camera?

to relax and enjoy yourself in a lazy way:
laze in
 We spent the afternoon lazing in the sun.
laze about/ around
 We lazed around, gazing at the views.

if something is lying around, it has been left somewhere in an untidy way, rather than being in its proper place:
 If you leave your shoes lying around like that, you'll trip over them.
 Papers and books lay around the room in complete chaos.
2if you lie around, you spend time lying down and not doing anything:
 I felt so lazy just lying around on the beach all day.

words.eng:
hang around/ round (something)
phrasal verb
informal

description.eng:
to wait or spend time somewhere, doing nothing:
 I hung around the station for an hour but he never came.

words.eng:
clear off
phrasal verb
BrE informal

description.eng:
to leave a place quickly:
 They cleared off when they saw the police coming.
clear off! (=used to tell someone angrily to go away)

read/discuss
to read or discuss something in order to make sure it is correct:
 We'll go through the details later on.
 Do you want me to go through this and check your spellings?

to experience difficult or dangerous conditions
SYN endure
 the generation that lived through the Second World War
 It was hard to describe the nightmare she had lived through.

to look at a book, magazine, set of photographs etc quickly:
 Will flicked through Carla's photo album.

(=look at the pages quickly)
I looked through the book until I found the right section.

1sleep through something
to sleep while something is happening and not be woken by it:
 How did you manage to sleep through that thunderstorm?
2sleep through (something)
to sleep continuously for a long time:
 I slept right through till lunchtime.
 The baby slept peacefully through the night.
I put on a lot of weight last year

to decide to stop working, especially because you have done enough or you are tired:
 Come on, let's call it a day and go home.

if two or more people take turns doing work, using something etc, they do it one after the other, for example in order to share the work or play fairly:
 You'll have to take turns on the swing.
take turns doing something
 The students were taking turns reading aloud.
take turns in doing somethingBrE:
 We took turns in pushing the bike along.
take turns to do something
 Dan and I usually take turns to cook.

if you write or draw something in rough, you do it without paying attention to details or tidiness, because you are going to do it again later:
 It's best to work in rough first, and then write it out neatly.

used to say that an action will not achieve what it is intended to achieve:
 It's no good telling him - he won't listen.

description.eng:
a small sign of a problem that is much larger:

increase or develop sth over a period of time (build up confidence / strength/a reputation, etc.).
practicing with classmates will help you to build up your confidence

words.eng: dos and don'ts (also do's and don'ts)

description.eng:
things that you should and should not do in a particular situation:

make what you say clear to other people, especially when speaking a foreign language)

if you do something by trial and error, you test many different methods of doing something in order to find the best

to get as much advantage as you can from a situation while you are able to:

when you know something by heart, you remember all of it exactly:

in such a way that people can hear you
SYN aloud

if you get behind with a job, payments, rent etc, you do not do or pay as much of it as you should have by a particular time:
get behind with

1to improve and reach the same standard as other people in your class, group etc:

catch up with

words.eng:
stick at something
phrasal verb
BrE

description.eng:
1to continue doing something in a determined way in order to achieve something:

achieve something after a period of time work or effort
don't give up. stick at it and you'll get there in the end

to understand something, especially something difficult or complicated:

to learn something by watching or listening to other people:

to keep paying attention to something, even though it is difficult:

keep your mind on the job/ task in/ at hand

to practise and improve your skills or your knowledge of something that you learned in the past:

to manage to do as much or as well as other people
OPP fall behind
keep up with

to try very hard to remember or think of something:

if something comes back to you, you remember it or remember how to do it:

come back to

words.eng: on the tip of your tongue
go in (at) one ear and out (at) the other informal

in complete control of a situation:

used to give advice or an opinion)
